title: How To Bathe a Cat
content: Most cats don’t need regular baths, but when they get dirty or have certain medical conditions, you can safely bathe them using gentle handling, cat-safe shampoo, and a calm environment. Cats are excellent self-groomers, which is why many pet parents rarely (if ever) need to bathe them. But sometimes, a bath becomes necessary—whether your cat got into something messy or your veterinarian recommends it for a skin condition. Here’s how to bathe a cat safely and with as little stress as possible.  Cats are often good at self-grooming and therefore need to be bathed only in certain circumstances, veterinarians say. For example, cats might need a bath when they’re dirty from getting into something or if they have any sort of medical issue that merits being bathed, such as skin problems. “Generally, other than for cleaning off substances, cats would only need to be bathed at the direction of a veterinarian,” says Sandra Mitchell, DVM, a Maine-based veterinarian.  Should you need to bathe your cat, you won’t need much in the way of supplies. Jennifer Coates, DVM, a veterinarian in Colorado, recommends just the essentials: No-slip mat (to place either in a wash basin or at the bottom of a deep sink) A moisturizingcat-safe shampoo, likeDouxo S3 Soothing Shampoo A cup for rinsing your cat A damp washcloth to clean your cat’s face Acat bath towel   If self-grooming isn’t getting the job done, your cat might need your help. Here’s how to bathe a cat effectively. If you’re bathing your cat in a sink, Dr. Coates recommends gently lowering your cat into the water to get their fur wet. You can remove your cat from the sink to shampoo them if it’s easier, and then put them back in the sink to be rinsed. Dr. Mitchell recommends using at least two basins: one for shampooing and one for rinsing. Melissa Stevens, owner ofThe Cat’s Pajamas Feline Exclusive Groomingin Fairfax Station, Virginia, recommends shampooing cats during a bath at least two and as many as three times to take care of greasy fur or if your cat is really dirty.  After the preliminary shampooing and rinsing, really get down to the cat’s skin with a second shampoo process. You’ll be able to tell when the cat is clean once there’s a creamy, “sudsy lather,” she says. If a cat is particularly dirty, Stevens recommends a third shampoo lather or, in the case ofdandruff, a medicated shampoo, likeDermaBenSs, for the bathing process.  If needed, wash your cat’s face with a damp towel, Dr. Coates says. It’s important to make sure your cat is completely dry at the end of the bathing process. Otherwise, their fur can easily become matted, Stevens says. Dry your cat thoroughly with a towel after their bath.  Here are some tips to make bathing your cat easier: Clip your cat’s nailswithcat nail clippersahead of bathing them, in case they scratch during the process.  Similarly, Dr. Mitchell recommends filling your water tubs ahead of time to avoid scaring your cat with running water. Help your cat get used to baths when they’re younger. Don’t be afraid to outsource the job to a professional cat groomer. “It’s best to leave baths to your veterinarian or professional groomer if they are so upsetting that you or your cat could be injured,” Dr. Coates says.  Vets don’t recommend bathing cats too often in part because it can dry out their skin. Dr. Coates says to avoid bathing cats more frequently than every four to six weeks. A bath can also be a stressful experience for your cat, Dr. Mitchell says, and that can ultimately lead to illnesses. “A bath along with a lion clip is one thing,” she says. “Bathing just to bathe is just not needed.” WRITTEN BY Allison Prang Freelance Writer
